blocks.content:
# Interactive Demo: Semantic Layer for Grafana

We'll quickly walk through how BI dashboards are enhanced by a Semantic Layer, both for dashboard viewers and authors.
With "Cross filtering" we can click on any panel-data, and filter the entire dashboard to that data value
Navigate to the **Grafana for BI: Semantic Layer** demo dashboard

Click to filter: show Credit-card payments only

Within the **Order Amount by Payment Method** panel, click the "credit_card" bar, then the **Filter for …** button in the tooltip

steps.description: Click one of the bars within the bar chart.

blocks.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Orders: Amount and Count, by First Name'] div[data-testid='data-testid panel content']
blocks.content: Now we'll click to filter from the Table panel. First hover a cell to show the filter buttons
Now click the "Add to filter" icon

blocks.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Orders: Amount, by First Name'] div[data-testid='uplot-main-div']
blocks.content: Notice how all other panels, including this one, are immediately filtered per the filters we've added.
blocks.tooltip: Notice how all other panels, including this one, are immediately filtered per the filters we've added.

blocks.content: Now let's further customise the filters we've added. We'll update `last_name` to filter for one of many values.
blocks.tooltip: Now let's further customise the filters we've added. We'll update `last_name` to filter for one of many values.

blocks.content: Navigate to the **Grafana for BI: Semantic Layer** demo dashboard
Let's look at how this is done, and see the SQL which is generated by the Semantic Layer.
First edit the **Orders: Amount, by First Name** panel

steps.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Orders: Amount, by First Name'] button[data-testid='data-testid Panel menu Orders: Amount, by First Name']

blocks.content:
Notice that only two selections were needed to build the query for this panel:
- a single Dimension and
- a single Measure
Yet this complete SQL Query was generated for us by the Semantic Layer data source
Let's remove our filters now, and see the conditions in the `WHERE` clause such as `` `payments`.payment_method `` disappear
blocks.tooltip: Let's remove our filters now, and see the conditions in the `WHERE` clause such as `` `payments`.payment_method `` disappear

blocks.content: Let's clear the variables.
You can now see the statement has been removed from the where clause.
blocks.tooltip: You can now see the statement has been removed from the where clause.

blocks.content: Navigate to the **Grafana for BI: Semantic Layer** demo dashboard

Drag a box to zoom in on a specific time range

Zoom in again to a smaller time range to see other panels filtered further

blocks.reftarget: input[data-testid='data-testid Dashboard template variables Variable Value DropDown value link text order_details.order_date-input']
blocks.content:
Notice the `order_details.order_date` field is being used as our Time-range filtering field.

It's this field which lands in the SQL `WHERE` clause, and which is filtered to the time-range start and end timestamps.

The Semantic Layer data source doesn't just convert Grafana AdHoc filters to SQL `WHERE` clauses. It will inject the necessary `JOIN`s too.  
  
This dynamic injection means we never hit any `column not found` error.
Let's first navigate to a more involved BI Dashboard - the Territory Navigator example

blocks.reftarget: div[data-viz-panel-key='panel-59679'] section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Open Opportunities (Cube)']
blocks.content: Scroll to the Open Opportunities (Cube) panel.
blocks.scrollContainer: div[data-testid='data-testid DashboardEditPaneSplitter primary body'] div[data-testid='data-testid DashboardEditPaneSplitter body container']

blocks.content:
filter to **Stage: Qualification**

See the rest of the dashboard update.
